title: Hidden culprit found for puzzling static electricity
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Turning cellular powerhouses into therapies, a comet falls apart, and more
Brought to you by Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology

Now accepting entries for the US$ 25,000
Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology
Application Deadline June 15, 2026
www.eppendorf.com/prize
View this email in your browser
19 March 2026
Today’s The Life Academic celebrates a new award for scientists who own up to their mistakes. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including a comet’s cosmic breakup and how to turn cellular powerhouses into therapies.
Physics  |  News from Science
Hidden culprit found for puzzling static electricity
Some kinds of static electricity are easy to understand. Rub a balloon against your hair, and negative charges will accumulate on the rubber because it has a greater affinity for holding charges. Your hair, now positively charged, will be attracted to the balloon. And because like charges repel, strands of your hair will splay out from each other.

But identical materials with identical affinities can also exchange charges, seemingly without rhyme or reason. Particles in volcanic ash plumes somehow build up enough charge to trigger lightning; dust in grain silos can spark and explode. In a study published yesterday in Nature, researchers say they have finally found the culprit: trace amounts of surface contamination by carbon-bearing molecules from the air.

This revelation resolves “one of the bigger questions in the field, a scientific mystery that has lasted for decades,” according to soft-matter physicist Justin Burton.
Read the full story
Astronomy  |  Icarus
This comet is never ever getting back together
The comet progressively disintegrated over the 3-day period—the first time Hubble has witnessed a comet so early in the process of breaking up.  D. Bodewits/NASA/ESA; J. DePasquale/STScI
While careening through the universe, every comet splinters up about once in 100 years. So it’s all the more shocking that scientists using the Hubble Space Telescope caught a cosmic break-up last November.

The comet, C/2025 K1 (ATLAS), shortened to K1, is now about 400 million kilometers away. But it was already heading out of the Solar System when researchers—hoping to target a different comet—caught it with Hubble. Through three 20-second images, they noticed that K1, which is a bit larger than average comets at 8 kilometers across, had fragmented into four chunks just days before. Each piece sported a hazy outline characteristic of gas and dust. “While I was taking an initial look at the data, I saw that there were four comets in those images when we only proposed to look at one,” explained  astrophysicist John Noonan in a statement.  “Right when we observed it, it happened to break apart, which is the slimmest of slim chances.”

Studying comet breakups can unearth scarce data about their interiors, which are more pristine than the outer casing that has been irradiated by the Sun and cosmic rays. Already, ground-based analyses have revealed that the comet’s composition is odd, with lower amounts of carbon than is typical. Researchers think K1 came to a shattering end similar to that of other comets that pass too close to the Sun: a vaporization of their icy interiors and an outward blast.

“This is the sort of thing that’s happening all the time in the solar system,” Noonan told The New York Times. But the odds of actually catching a break-up on a telescope are “vanishingly small.”
Read the paper
Medicine  |  News from Science
Turning cellular powerhouses into therapies
It could be the ultimate energy boost. Researchers say they have developed a new way to transplant healthy mitochondria, the power stations of our cells, into mice harboring defective versions—a strategy the team hopes could one day treat certain neurodegenerative disorders and inherited genetic diseases in people.

Faulty mitochondria are a major cause of disease, and researchers have long sought ways to introduce healthy versions into muscle or other tissue. But in previous efforts that injected free-floating mitochondria, these organelles were seldom taken up by recipient cells, and they got damaged during or after the journey.

The new technique, described this week in Cell, packages mitochondria into microscopic capsules formed from membranes of red blood cells. Injections of these capsules improved symptoms in mice replicating features of Parkinson’s disease and other conditions. “It strikes me as a long way from ready ” for use in people, cautioned Ken Nakamura, a physician-scientist studying Parkinson’s. Furthermore, the mechanism underlying the benefits in animals isn’t entirely clear—“but it’s certainly an exciting idea.”
Read the full story
SPONSORED
Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology: Call for Entries 2026
This prize is awarded to young scientists for their outstanding contributions to neurobiological research based on experimental methods of molecular, cellular, systems, or organismic biology. Researchers not older than 35 years are invited to apply.
Apply Now
The Life Academic
Yrithinnd via WIKIMEDIA COMMONS  |  CC BY-SA
New prize rewards scientists who set the record straight
Phie Jacobs, General Assignment Reporter, News from Science
Since its founding in 2010, Retraction Watch has chronicled numerous instances of blatant scientific misconduct. But the blog also highlights stories of researchers who do the right thing by retracting their own work after noticing errors. “We wish there were more of them to report,” publication cofounder Ivan Oransky tells ScienceAdviser. “But we also know how hard it is to come forward about mistakes.”

That’s why the Center for Scientific Integrity, the nonprofit organization behind Retraction Watch, has launched a new annual award celebrating scientists who discover substantial errors in their published work and take meaningful steps to correct the scientific record. The Ctrl-Z Award, named after the computer keyboard shortcut for the “undo” command, is funded by donors Harvey Motulsky and Earl Beutler. It aims to alleviate some of the stigma associated with acknowledging scientific errors.

Oransky, who serves as the center’s executive director, says the judges will be looking for nominees who were proactive and honest in their attempts to correct the scientific record despite the “reputational risk” involved. He highlights the case of behavioral ecologist Kate Laskowski, who posted openly on social media after retracting a 2016 study she’d co-authored with prominent spider researcher David Pruitt. A university investigation later found evidence that Pruitt had falsified and fabricated data in multiple other papers.

The Ctrl-Z Award, worth $2500, features categories for both early-career and senior researchers. As Oransky explains, retracting a paper “can be life-changing” for a scientist who’s just starting out. Neuroscientist Benjamin Haas, who had just launched his own lab when he learned of data analysis problems in one of his published papers, immediately contacted the journal to ask for a retraction despite worries about his career. As Haas tells Nature, support from his former PhD advisor helped make the process less intimidating.

For more established researchers, Oransky says, “it’s about your long-term reputation.” Although multiple Nobel laureates have retracted papers, some have been more candid than others. Genetics researcher Gregg Semenza, who shared the 2019 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, has attracted controversy after more than a dozen retractions, which he largely declined to discuss publicly. By contrast, chemical engineer Frances Arnold, who won the Nobel Prize for Chemistry in 2018, publicly announced that she was retracting a paper published in Science when her team was unable to replicate the results.

Oransky also notes that the risks associated with retractions may not be as bad as they appear. Recent studies suggest that when scientists self-report errors, their work continues to be cited, and some researchers have even garnered praise for their willingness to admit mistakes. “That’s reassuring,” Oransky says. “I do feel like the culture has shifted to some extent.”
Et Cetera
All by itself
The preprint server arXiv.org has decided to strike out on its own; after more than 20 years of being hosted by Cornell University, the site will become an independent nonprofit corporation this summer. Some are concerned the move could ultimately result in a for-profit enterprise if arXiv struggles to obtain enough funding. “But long-term sustainability has always been a concern,” said founder Paul Ginsparg. “If they think arXiv has been stable, they don’t know to what extent it was the Perils of Pauline all along”—a reference to a 1914 film melodrama—because the service has been run “on a shoestring.”
Read more at ScienceInsider
Scrutinizing tradition
New regulations in China will demand evidence of efficacy from injected traditional Chinese medicines (TCMs)—or pull them from the market. The new rules “mark a fundamental shift in the development paradigm of TCM injections and even the entire TCM industry from relying on traditional experience to embracing modern scientific evidence,” said one expert.
Read more at ScienceInsider
Turing Award goes quantum
The biggest prize in computer science, the A.M. Turing Award, has for the first time honored work in the quantum realm. The $1 million award is shared by physicist Charles Bennett and computer scientist Gilles Brassard for “their essential role in establishing the foundations of quantum information science.” The prize is more for potential than accomplishment, one expert said. “They’re sending a message. That message is that this is an important area, we need to focus attention on it, and it’s going to have a big impact in the future.”
Read more at ScienceInsider
It is reasonable to expect that researchers will eventually uncover the mechanisms of emotions for self-regulation and social interaction. When they do, they may have to confront the dilemma of whether a strict adherence to neurobiology will doom robots to be unhappy.
Focus  |  18 March 2026  |  Robin R. Murphy
Last but not least
I love a feel-good conservation story; a sound-good one is even better.
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Eric Hand, Hannah Richter, and Kelly Servick

raw_metadata.subject: Hidden culprit found for puzzling static electricity
